source.title: Catastrophic Rupture of Heat Exchanger (Seven Fatalities) – Tesoro Anacortes Refinery Investigation Report

actions_taken: Post-incident metallurgical analysis of B and E heat exchangers conducted by BETA Laboratory and NIST
CSB computer modeling of NHT heat exchanger process conditions performed
Tesoro installed new NHT heat exchangers with upgraded HTHA-resistant materials of construction post-incident
New single-bank heat exchanger design implemented to eliminate online switching hazard
Advanced process control system installed to minimize fouling
Additional instrumentation installed on replacement heat exchangers
Tesoro issued new PHA procedure in 2012 removing limiting assumptions
Washington L&I DOSH issued 39 willful and 5 serious citations to Tesoro post-incident
NEP inspection formally closed March 2009 with 17 process safety code violations identified
event.summary: On April 2, 2010, at 12:30 a.m., the E heat exchanger (E-6600E) in the Naphtha Hydrotreater (NHT) unit of the Tesoro Anacortes Refinery catastrophically ruptured due to High Temperature Hydrogen Attack (HTHA). The rupture released highly flammable hydrogen and naphtha at over 500°F, which autoignited causing an explosion and intense fire lasting more than three hours. Seven Tesoro employees (one shift supervisor and six operators) who were performing startup of the adjacent A/B/C heat exchanger bank were fatally injured. HTHA had severely degraded the carbon steel shell of the E heat exchanger over 38 years of service, accumulating in high-stress non-post-weld-heat-treated weld zones. Contributing organizational factors included: normalization of hazardous flange leaks during startup, failure of PHAs to identify HTHA as a credible hazard for the shell-side of the B and E exchangers, reliance on design operating conditions rather than actual measured conditions for HTHA susceptibility assessments, inadequate process instrumentation between heat exchangers, and failure to limit personnel exposure during hazardous nonroutine startup work. The carbon steel Nelson curve (API RP 941) was found to be inaccurate, with HTHA occurring up to 120°F below the predicted safe operating boundary.
recommendations: EPA to revise 40 CFR Part 68 to require documented use of inherently safer systems analysis and hierarchy of controls (R1)
EPA to enforce inherently safer systems analysis through Clean Air Act General Duty Clause until R1 is in effect (R2)
EPA to develop guidance for inherently safer systems analysis enforcement (R3)
EPA to participate in Tesoro Anacortes process safety culture survey oversight committee (R4)
Washington State Legislature to augment PSM regulations with goal-setting attributes including ALARP, DMHR requirements, hierarchy of controls, and workforce participation (R5)
Washington State Legislature to establish well-funded technically qualified regulator (R6)
Washington State Legislature to develop process safety leading and lagging indicators program (R7)
Washington DOSH to perform verification audits at all Washington petroleum refineries for HTHA prevention and nonroutine work controls (R8)
Washington DOSH to participate in Tesoro process safety culture oversight committee (R9)

API to revise API RP 941 to establish minimum 'shall' requirements, require inherently safer materials, verify actual operating conditions, and prohibit carbon steel above 400°F and 50 psia hydrogen partial pressure (R10)
API to revise API RP 581 to establish minimum 'shall' requirements, prohibit carbon steel in HTHA-susceptible service, and require verification of actual operating conditions (R11)
Tesoro Refining to actively participate with API in RP 941 revisions (R12)
Tesoro Refining to develop and implement plan to meet revised API RP 941 requirements (R13)
Tesoro Refining to revise and improve PHA, IOW, and DMHR programs and cross-linking (R14)
Tesoro Anacortes Refinery to implement process safety culture continuous improvement program with tripartite oversight committee (R15)
United Steelworkers Local 12-591 to effectively participate in process safety culture oversight committee (R16)

hazards.name: Carbon steel heat exchanger containing hydrogen and naphtha at high temperature and pressure in HTHA-susceptible service
Flammable hydrocarbon and hydrogen inventory in NHT heat exchanger system above autoignition temperature

threats.name: High Temperature Hydrogen Attack (HTHA) degradation of carbon steel heat exchanger shell
Non-post-weld heat-treated welds creating high-stress zones susceptible to HTHA accumulation
Heat exchanger fouling causing elevated shell-side temperatures exceeding design conditions
Hazardous nonroutine startup of heat exchanger bank with excessive personnel in hazard zone
Flange leaks of flammable hydrocarbons during heat exchanger startup

consequences.name: Seven worker fatalities from explosion and fire following catastrophic heat exchanger rupture
Catastrophic rupture of E heat exchanger releasing hot hydrogen and naphtha
Explosion and intense fire burning for more than three hours
Worker exposure to flammable hydrocarbon leaks and fire during startup operations

controls.name: API RP 941 Nelson Curve – Material Selection and HTHA Operating Limits
controls.barrier_role: Material selection guidance to prevent HTHA by operating below Nelson curve limits
controls.lod_basis: Industry standard for predicting HTHA occurrence based on temperature and hydrogen partial pressure

supporting_text: The CSB performed computer reconstruction of the process conditions within the NHT heat exchangers. The results of the computer reconstruction show that the portion of the carbon steel E heat exchanger that ruptured was estimated to have operated below the applicable Nelson curve. This was considered the safe region of operation where HTHA could not occur. Therefore, the carbon steel Nelson curve methodology is inaccurate, cannot be depended on to prevent HTHA equipment failures.
The coldest region in the E heat exchanger with identified HTHA was estimated to operate up to 120 ⁰F below the carbon steel Nelson curve.

controls.name: Damage Mechanism Hazard Reviews (DMHRs) / Corrosion Reviews
controls.barrier_role: Periodic review to identify HTHA and other damage mechanisms in process equipment
controls.lod_basis: Corrosion reviews conducted in 1990, 1999, 2003, and 2008 to assess HTHA susceptibility

supporting_text: None of these DMHRs conducted in the 20 years before the incident identified the potential danger of HTHA in the B and E heat exchangers because they primarily relied on design data instead of measured process conditions.

Shell Oil and Tesoro periodically performed damage mechanism hazard reviews (DMHRs), called corrosion reviews. However, these reviews did not identify HTHA as a credible failure mechanism for the B and E heat exchangers. These reviews were weakened by primarily relying on design operating parameters for these heat exchangers rather than data from actual process operating conditions.

controls.name: Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) Program
controls.barrier_role: Systematic identification, evaluation, and control of process hazards including HTHA and nonroutine work hazards
controls.lod_basis: PSM-required PHA conducted in 1996 (Shell Oil), revalidated 2001 and 2006, new PHA 2010

supporting_text: The PSM-required PHAs conducted on the NHT heat exchangers failed to prevent the April 2010 incident or to reduce the consequences by limiting personnel access to potentially dangerous areas during the hazardous startup activity.
The 2010 Tesoro NHT unit PHA failed to identify HTHA as a hazard for the shell of the B and E heat exchangers.

For the 15 years before the April 2010 incident, assumptions used by PHA teams at the Anacortes refinery contributed to ineffective safeguards, ineffective hazard identification, and ineffective control of hazards to prevent equipment failures from HTHA damage.

controls.name: HTHA Inspection Program
controls.barrier_role: Nondestructive examination of heat exchangers to detect HTHA damage before failure
controls.lod_basis: Mechanical integrity inspection program cited as safeguard in PHAs; HTHA inspection required if operating within 25°F or 25 psia of Nelson curve

supporting_text: The B and E heat exchangers were never inspected for HTHA and more HTHA-resistant materials were never considered until after the April 2010 incident.
Inspection is therefore not sufficiently reliable to ensure mechanical integrity and prevent HTHA equipment damage.
Successful identification of HTHA is highly dependent on the specific techniques employed and the skill of the inspector, and there are few inspectors who have this expertise.

controls.name: Process Temperature and Pressure Instrumentation on NHT Heat Exchanger Banks
controls.barrier_role: Monitoring of operating conditions to detect approach to HTHA-susceptible conditions and trigger inspection
controls.lod_basis: Temperature and pressure indicators at bank inlet/outlet; no intermediate instrumentation between individual exchangers

supporting_text: Although some temperature and pressure measurements were taken surrounding the NHT heat exchanger banks, no temperature measurements were made between the heat exchangers. Thus, Tesoro did not know the operating temperature of the process fluid entering and exiting the B and E heat exchangers.
No temperature instrumentation was on the B or E heat exchangers.

controls.name: Inherently Safer Materials of Construction (HTHA-resistant alloys)
controls.barrier_role: Use of chromium or stainless steel alloys resistant to HTHA to eliminate damage mechanism
controls.lod_basis: API RP 571 identifies 300 Series SS, 5Cr, 9Cr, 12Cr alloys as not susceptible to HTHA at refinery conditions

supporting_text: The B and E heat exchangers were not constructed from these inherently safer materials.
Inherently safer design is a better approach to prevent HTHA. API has identified high chromium steels that are significantly more resistant to HTHA than carbon steel.
Both the Chevron and Tesoro incidents could have been prevented if inherently safer equipment construction materials had been used.

controls.name: Post-Weld Heat Treatment (PWHT) of Heat Exchanger Welds
controls.barrier_role: Reduce residual stress in weld heat-affected zones to modestly improve HTHA resistance
controls.lod_basis: PWHT reduces residual stress and makes carbon less reactive with hydrogen; ASME Boiler Code did not require PWHT for B and E exchangers

supporting_text: The welds in the B and E heat exchanger shells were not post-weld heat-treated. As a result, the heat-affected zones illustrated in Figure 19 were likely high-stress areas where HTHA damage ultimately accumulated.
Post-weld heat treating is a manual activity and therefore low on the hierarchy of controls.

controls.name: Stainless Steel Cladding on Can 4 of B and E Heat Exchangers
controls.barrier_role: Reduce effective hydrogen partial pressure on carbon steel in hottest section to limit HTHA susceptibility
controls.lod_basis: Type 316 SS cladding applied to Can 4 (hottest section) only; originally intended for sulfidation corrosion protection

supporting_text: The stainless steel cladding reduces the effective hydrogen partial pressure that is acting on the carbon steel beneath the cladding.

The stainless-steel-clad portion of the B and E heat exchangers was estimated to occasionally operate above the carbon steel Nelson curve. No HTHA was found in this region, likely because stainless steel cladding reduced the potential for HTHA in the carbon steel beneath it.
The remaining portions of the exchanger shell (Cans 1, 2, and 3) did not have a 316 stainless steel cladding.

controls.name: Management of Change (MOC) Program
controls.barrier_role: Evaluate safety implications of process and organizational changes to prevent introduction of new hazards
controls.lod_basis: Tesoro MOC policy including Management of Organizational Change (MOOC) requirements

supporting_text: Tesoro routinely relied on additional staff members during NHT heat exchanger startups but never assessed the risks or made any attempts to control them. Tesoro did not conduct an MOC to consider the risks of these organizational changes, despite its policy that required the performance of such a risk assessment.

MOCs Did not Effectively Control Hazardous Conditions. The Tesoro MOC policy states, 'Management of Change helps ensure that changes to a process do not inadvertently introduce new hazards or unknowingly increase the risk of existing hazards.' However, Tesoro decided that a hazard evaluation of the addition of steam stations was not required under their procedures.

controls.name: NHT Heat Exchanger Startup Procedure
controls.barrier_role: Step-by-step task list controlling startup sequence, valve operations, and personnel roles to safely return heat exchangers to service
controls.lod_basis: Written startup procedure specifying one outside operator; used by inside board operator and outside operator

supporting_text: The heat exchanger startup procedure specifies the use of only one outside operator to perform startup operations of the NHT heat exchanger banks. However on the day of the incident, a supervisor requested five additional operators to assist with the startup of the A/B/C heat exchanger bank.
The startup procedure did not specify defined roles for these six additional personnel.
Tesoro procedures did not prohibit or effectively limit the use of additional personnel during the nonroutine hazardous startup of the NHT heat exchangers.

controls.name: Pressure Relief Valve on Downstream Vessel
controls.barrier_role: Protect heat exchanger from overpressure by relieving at 585 psig set pressure
controls.lod_basis: Pressure relief valve set at 585 psig protecting NHT heat exchanger system

supporting_text: The E heat exchanger was protected from excessive pressure by a pressure relief valve on a downstream vessel, which was set to relieve the pressure at 585 psig. Operating data indicate that the pressure relief valve was not challenged and did not open before the incident. The relief valve was inspected and tested after the incident, and it opened at the designated set pressure.

controls.name: Steam Lances for Leak Mitigation During Startup
controls.barrier_role: Suppress and mitigate hydrocarbon leaks and incipient fires during heat exchanger startup
controls.lod_basis: Steam lances staged and ready for use during startup; steam stations installed October 2009 MOC

supporting_text: Four steam lances were staged and ready for use during the startup to mitigate any leaks or fires that might occur.
Relying on steam suppression to mitigate leaks during NHT heat exchanger startups was a common practice and was part of the startup procedure.
In October 2009, Tesoro approved a Management of Change (MOC) to install two new permanent steam stations near the NHT heat exchangers.

controls.name: Emergency Response and Alarm System
controls.barrier_role: Alert personnel and activate emergency response to contain and control fire following loss of containment
controls.lod_basis: Refinery alarm and emergency response team activation protocols

supporting_text: During the 2009 incident, the refinery alarm was not sounded; an emergency response team was not activated; the leak was not isolated from the unit; and the unit was not shut down.
The TOP investigation team noted complacency at the refinery because these events were so common and also cited a growing lack of concern toward activating emergency response.

controls.name: Access Control and Personnel Minimization During Hazardous Nonroutine Work
controls.barrier_role: Limit number of personnel exposed to hazards during nonroutine startup operations
controls.lod_basis: CCPS guidance to minimize number of people in harm's way; startup procedure specifying single outside operator

supporting_text: Tesoro procedures did not prohibit or effectively limit the use of additional personnel during the nonroutine hazardous startup of the NHT heat exchangers.
On the day of the incident, a supervisor requested five additional operators to assist with the startup of the A/B/C heat exchanger bank.
Seven workers were performing the role that procedurally was intended for a single outside operator.

controls.name: OSHA NEP Regulatory Inspection of NHT Unit
controls.barrier_role: Independent regulatory verification of PSM compliance including mechanical integrity of heat exchangers
controls.lod_basis: Washington DOSH NEP audit of Tesoro CR/NHT unit conducted October 2008 – March 2009

supporting_text: The heat exchanger that failed, the E heat exchanger, was a fundamental component of the Tesoro NEP audit. However, no citable mechanical integrity or other process safety management deficiencies related to the heat exchanger were found.
The NEP team lead confirmed that little emphasis was placed on possible damage mechanisms that could be present, including HTHA.
None of the team members had an engineering or metallurgy background, and the team as a whole had limited experience with PSM and with refinery operations.

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
notes.analyst_notes: PIF references: PIF-001 = competence/expertise of corrosion experts and inspectors in HTHA assessment; PIF-002 = procedures inadequate for nonroutine work hazard control; PIF-003 = safety culture normalization of hazardous conditions and supervision failures; PIF-004 = workload driving need for additional operators. The incident involved multiple simultaneous barrier failures across technical, organizational, and regulatory dimensions. The carbon steel Nelson curve (C-001) failed as a predictive tool. HTHA inspection (C-004) was never performed on B/E exchangers despite being cited as a PHA safeguard. Inherently safer materials (C-006) and PWHT (C-007) were not implemented. The stainless steel cladding on Can 4 (C-008) functioned as intended and prevented HTHA in that section. The pressure relief valve (C-011) functioned correctly but was irrelevant to the HTHA failure mode. Seven fatalities resulted from the combination of catastrophic equipment failure and excessive personnel in the hazard zone.
